data.title: Hims & Hers doubles down on estrogen patches as demand surges
data.description:
Hims & Hers Health (HIMS) on Wednesday announced that women experiencing perimenopause and menopause can secure a reliable supply of estrogen patches through its platform as demand for the hormone replacement therapy rises in the U.S.

The San Francisco, California-based Health Tech said that eligible patients will be able to buy estrogen patch kits starting at $134 per month via its Hers platform. The company will also sell prescription-based progesterone as part of its menopause and perimenopause offerings.

“With 1.3 million women entering menopause annually, the outdated supply chain can’t keep pace with a new generation of women ready to reclaim their health and vitality,” Hims (HIMS) said in a statement. “For any woman seeking estrogen patches during this crisis, Hers is here to help.”

According to data analytics firm Truveta, estrogen-based hormone replacement therapies such as pills and patches indicated for menopause symptoms like hot flashes climbed ~19% through February after the FDA championed their use in July.

Following the recommendation of an expert advisory panel convened in July, the FDA said in November that it will remove broad “black box” warnings associated with HRT products used for menopause symptoms.

“The FDA’s removal of the Black Box warning was a clear victory for science over stigma. But it also exposed a system in crisis,” HIMS said, adding, “Hers is stepping into this gap to ensure that in this moment of uncertainty, women experiencing perimenopause and menopause have a reliable health partner.”

data.title: Philip Morris rallies and lifts tobacco peers on IQOS strength, consumer resiliency
data.description:
Philip Morris International (PM) gained 6.9% in Wednesday afternoon trading following the release of its Q1 earnings report and gave a lift to the tobacco sector in general.

A key pullout from the report is that Philip Morris (PM) has not seen a "discernible shift in consumer behavior" from the Middle East conflict amid consumer discretionary spending pressures. The company noted that its full-year forecast does not factor in a prolonged impact from the Middle East situation but allows for some increases in energy, transport, and other key costs.

For Q1, Philip Morris (PM) reported strong smoke-free growth, especially IQOS, while the U.S. ZYN business was softer because of channel inventory normalization and a tougher competitive backdrop. Of note, the international smoke-free revenue grew 24.7% organically, and gross profit rose 28.6% organically.

Across the tobacco-related sector, RLX Technology (RLX) was up 4.5%, Ispire Technology (ISPR) gained 4.2%, Turning Point Brands (TPB) rose 2.9%, and Altria (MO) was 1.1% higher.

data.title: Roth Capital expects the rally in CAVA Group to extend
data.description:
Roth Capital started off coverage on CAVA Group (CAVA) with a Buy rating and price target of $106.  Analyst JP Wollam and his team think there is even more upside for the restaurant stock after the healthy 2026 outlook set up a rally. Wollam noted that the guidance was better than fast-casual peers and may be on the conservative side.  

"We think guidance that implies a meaningful deceleration following 1Q may be conservative given multi-year stacked traffic trends and protein innovation, loyalty, and throughput initiatives in the remainder of the year," he noted. "Additionally, our cohort analysis rebuts the view that comp growth is increasingly dependent on new unit openings, as the outlook points to mature units (>3 years) comping over 5% in 2026," he added.

Notably, CAVA's (CAVA) brand is seen compounding ahead of its physical footprint, which gave Roth Capital confidence to model nearly 1,000 units for 2030 and almost $500M of EBITDA annually. Working backwards, the firm assigned a one-year price target of $106 on CAVA but envisions a share price of $137 in three years by utilizing a 32X multiple that reflects further whitespace.

The Wall Street analyst scorecard on CAVA Group (CAVA) shows 16 Buy-equivalent ratings stacking up against 12 Hold-equivalent ratings and 1 Sell-equivalent rating.

Shares of CAVA (CAVA) were down 0.3% in Wednesday afternoon trading. The restaurant stock is up more than 60% on a year-to-date basis.

data.title: Moody's outlines ~$2.5B 2026 buybacks while holding full-year guidance amid volatility
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Moody's (MCO) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "Q1 was a strong start to the year despite a volatile geopolitical backdrop" and "Moody's again delivered sustained revenue growth across both businesses and powerful operating leverage" (President, CEO & Director Robert Fauber).
  • "Both MIS and MA grew revenues by 8%" and "disciplined cost management drove 150 basis points of adjusted operating margin to 53.2%"; management highlighted "adjusted diluted EPS of $4.33" and said it "returned $1.7 billion through buybacks and dividends" while "increased full year buyback guidance by $500 million to approximately $2.5 billion" (President, CEO & Director Fauber).
  • On Ratings demand, management said "in the first quarter, rated issuance surpassed $2 trillion for the first time" and highlighted "private credit-related revenue in Ratings growing more than 80% year-over-year" (President, CEO & Director Fauber).
  • On distribution into enterprise AI, management said "through Model Context Protocol integrations, Moody's licensed intelligence can now be accessed directly within enterprise AI environments such as ChatGPT Enterprise and Claude" and emphasized "these are bring-your-own license models" that "preserve our direct relationship with our customer" (President, CEO & Director Fauber).
  • "I am absolutely thrilled that Christina Kosmowski will become Moody's Analytics CEO in June" and management thanked "Andy Frepp for stepping up to serve as the Interim President" (President, CEO & Director Fauber).
  • "MA revenue increased 8% in the first quarter as reported or 6% on an organic constant currency basis" and "ARR ended Q1 at $3.6 billion, up 8% year-over-year" (Senior VP & CFO Noemie Heuland).

Outlook

  • "Looking ahead, our full year guidance remains unchanged across revenue, adjusted operating margin and adjusted diluted EPS" (Senior VP & CFO Heuland).
  • "For the second quarter, we expect MIS revenue growth in the low to mid-teens with adjusted diluted EPS of approximately $4.15 to $4.30" (Senior VP & CFO Heuland).
  • "If volatility persists beyond April" management said it "would expect full year MIS revenue growth to moderate to the mid-single-digit range with adjusted diluted EPS trending towards the low end of our guidance range" (Senior VP & CFO Heuland).
  • "For MA, we expect to close the sale of our Regulatory Solutions business on April 30" and said it has been "excluded" from "our reported revenue outlook" while "this does not change our expectations for ARR or organic constant currency recurring revenue growth" (Senior VP & CFO Heuland).

Financial Results

  • "Both MIS and MA grew revenues by 8%" and consolidated "adjusted operating margin" was "53.2%" with "adjusted diluted EPS of $4.33" (President, CEO & Director Fauber).
  • MA detail included "Recurring revenue grew 11%" and "represented 98% of total MA revenue" while "transactional revenue declined materially, down 54% year-over-year" (Senior VP & CFO Heuland).
  • Profitability and cash/capital return items included "MA adjusted operating margin was 32.5%" and "MIS delivered an adjusted operating margin of 66.7%"; management also reported "free cash flow of $844 million" and reiterated it "returned approximately $1.7 billion to shareholders" while guiding "approximately $2.5 billion of share buybacks in 2026" (Senior VP & CFO Heuland).

Q&A

  • Keen Fai Tong, Goldman Sachs Group, Inc., Research Division: "Can you discuss how many customers are accessing Moody's data through these channels and what your plans are to monetize MCP distribution?" President, CEO & Director Fauber: "I would say, a number of large financial institutions who are trialing... our agent-ready data" and "it's in early days" with management looking "to convert those to... sales, through the balance of the year."
  • Scott Wurtzel, Wolfe Research, LLC: asked how much MIS leverage is driven by "technology innovations and AI efficiencies"; Senior VP & CFO Heuland said automation covers work "that precede the ratings committee" and "now we're adding AI to those workflows" including "financial statement spreading" and "data gathering."
  • Jeffrey Silber, BMO Capital Markets Equity Research: asked about "pull forward" and delays; President, CEO & Director Fauber said "there was no more pull forward than what we would consider to be within typical ranges" and noted "There is a backlog of Q1 deals... deferred into the second quarter" with "some optimism" for "May and June."
  • Andrew Nicholas, William Blair & Company L.L.C., Research Division: asked if regulators are a gating factor for AI; President, CEO & Director Fauber said regulators show "heightened sensitivity... around the use of AI to actually be making decisions" and emphasized "a very strong control environment" and customer expectations for "auditability."
  • Jason Haas, Wells Fargo Securities, LLC, Research Division: asked why ARR was better than expected; President, CEO & Director Fauber said "we had good sales execution" and "no change" to full-year thinking; Senior VP & CFO Heuland added "We had some swing deals that we were able to close."
  • Shlomo Rosenbaum, Stifel, Nicolaus & Company, Incorporated, Research Division: asked about guidance incorporating "the war in Iran"; President, CEO & Director Fauber said "the Iran war is the most important variable" and pointed to issuance windowing, noting "in March, 80% of investment-grade issuance was in 6 days" and concluded "we think it's most prudent to hold to our current guidance."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly negative to neutral, with questions pressing on monetization timing ("how many customers" on MCP), sustainability of margins and issuance timing, and scenario framing around geopolitics ("how did you incorporate the war in Iran").
  • Management’s tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks ("strong start," "delivered the strongest quarter on record") and more measured in Q&A, frequently using early-stage framing such as "it's in early days" (President, CEO & Director Fauber) and conditional language around volatility ("If volatility persists beyond April" (Senior VP & CFO Heuland)).
  • Versus the prior quarter’s emphasis on being "well positioned and confident," the current call added more explicit turbulence caveats, including "volatility may affect timing" (President, CEO & Director Fauber) and a defined downside case tied to April volatility (Senior VP & CFO Heuland).

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The prior quarter introduced 2026 guidance and positioned AI distribution as an expanding channel (including references to MCPs and AI portals); the current quarter shifted to execution examples, stating MCP access "can now be accessed directly" in tools like "ChatGPT Enterprise and Claude" and describing trials with large financial institutions (President, CEO & Director Fauber).
  • Portfolio-shaping remained consistent, moving from discussing divestitures in Q4 to a near-term close date in Q1: "we expect to close the sale of our Regulatory Solutions business on April 30" (Senior VP & CFO Heuland).
  • Capital return messaging intensified: Q4 framed intent to be "aggressively buying back shares"; Q1 raised authorization expectations, saying it "increased full year buyback guidance... to approximately $2.5 billion" (President, CEO & Director Fauber).
  • Analyst focus moved from broader AI moat concerns and MA portfolio pruning in Q4 to more detailed mechanics in Q1, including MCP monetization, AI efficiency impacts in MIS, and concrete calendarization of issuance/revenue expectations.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management repeatedly flagged market instability: "volatility may affect timing" for issuance (President, CEO & Director Fauber) and said its base case assumes "current market turbulence is largely contained to April" (Senior VP & CFO Heuland).
  • A defined downside case was provided: "If volatility persists beyond April" the company said it would expect "full year MIS revenue growth to moderate to the mid-single-digit range" (Senior VP & CFO Heuland).
  • Regulatory and governance scrutiny around AI was highlighted as a constraint on speed of adoption, with regulators showing "heightened sensitivity... around the use of AI to actually be making decisions" and customers requiring "auditability" and the ability to "audit our products" (President, CEO & Director Fauber).

Final Takeaway

Moody’s management framed Q1 as a "strong start" with "8%" revenue growth in both businesses, higher profitability, and increased 2026 buyback plans to "approximately $2.5 billion" while keeping full-year guidance unchanged. Leadership emphasized structural issuance drivers (including "rated issuance surpassed $2 trillion") and accelerating workflow-embedded distribution into enterprise AI environments, but paired that with explicit conditionality on geopolitical and market volatility, stating its base case assumes disruption is "largely contained to April" and outlining how prolonged volatility would shift MIS growth and EPS toward the low end of the full-year range.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Enbridge loses Supreme Court ruling in Michigan pipeline case
data.description:
The U.S. Supreme Court on Wednesday ruled against Enbridge's (ENB) request to transfer an environmental lawsuit related to Michigan's Line 5 from state court to federal court, in a win for state officials and opponents of the pipeline.

The justices ruled unanimously that Enbridge (ENB) had waited too long to seek to move the lawsuit by Michigan's attorney general to federal court, a venue generally considered more friendly to defendants.

The court said Enbridge (ENB) had 30 days to try to move the case to federal court but instead waited ​887 days to do so until after developments in related litigation over the pipeline.

The decision may revive a seven-year-old case brought by the Michigan attorney general to effectively block Enbridge (ENB) from using the 645-mile pipeline system that runs from Wisconsin through Michigan and into Ontario.

Enbridge (ENB) claimed the lawsuit should go to federal court in part because Michigan Gov. Whitmer's order conflicted with a 1977 pipeline transit treaty between the U.S. and Canada that bars state and local officials from halting fuel flows across the border.

data.title: Boston Scientific forecasts 2026 organic growth of 6.5% to 8% amid revised outlook for EP and WATCHMAN
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Boston Scientific (BSX) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "First quarter represented a solid quarter for Boston Scientific with total company organic sales growth of 9.4% versus our guidance range of 8.5% to 10%." (Chairman, President & CEO Michael Mahoney)
  • "2026 has proven to be a more challenging year than we initially expected." (Chairman, President & CEO Mahoney)
  • "We are guiding to organic growth of 5% to 7% for the second quarter and reducing our full year guidance to 6.5% to 8%, reflecting unanticipated headwinds and changing business patterns." (Chairman, President & CEO Mahoney)
  • "Last year, we did announce our intent to discontinue the POLARx cryo catheter, but have accelerated that timing given some recent safety events and the availability of nonthermal ablation technologies." (Chairman, President & CEO Mahoney)
  • "Urology did have a difficult quarter in Q1 as sales grew 1% organically, falling short of our expectations, driven primarily by the stone management and sacral neuromodulation businesses." (Chairman, President & CEO Mahoney)
  • "WATCHMAN grew 19% organically in the first quarter, which was below our expectations with pressure on volumes in the U.S. as the quarter progressed." (Chairman, President & CEO Mahoney)
  • "Organic sales grew 22%, 18% in the U.S. and 30% internationally." (Chairman, President & CEO Mahoney)
  • "First quarter consolidated revenue of $5.203 billion represents 11.6% reported growth versus first quarter 2025 and includes a 220 basis point tailwind from foreign exchange." (Executive VP & CFO Jonathan Monson)

Outlook

  • "We are guiding to organic growth of 5% to 7% for the second quarter" and "reducing our full year guidance to 6.5% to 8%." (Chairman, President & CEO Mahoney)
  • "Our second quarter '26 adjusted EPS guide is $0.82 to $0.84, and we now expect our full year adjusted EPS to be $3.34 to $3.41." (Chairman, President & CEO Mahoney)
  • "We now expect full year 2026 reported revenue growth to be in the range of 7.0% to 8.5% versus 2025" and "full year 2026 operational and organic growth to be in the range of 6.5% to 8.0%." (Executive VP & CFO Monson)
  • "We expect second quarter 2026 operational and organic growth to be in the range of 5.0% to 7.0%." (Executive VP & CFO Monson)
  • "We now expect full year 2026 adjusted earnings per share to be in the range of $3.34 to $3.41" and "we expect second quarter adjusted earnings per share to be in the range of $0.82 to $0.84." (Executive VP & CFO Monson)
  • Compared with the prior quarter’s call, management moved from "10% to 11%" full-year organic growth guidance and "$3.43 to $3.49" full-year adjusted EPS guidance to "6.5% to 8.0%" organic growth and "$3.34 to $3.41" adjusted EPS. (Executive VP & CFO Monson)

Financial Results

  • $0.80 adjusted EPS vs. the company’s guidance range of $0.78 to $0.80. (Chairman, President & CEO Mahoney)
  • "Adjusted gross margin for the first quarter was 70.5%, which represents a 100 basis point decline versus the first quarter of 2025, primarily driven by tariffs as well as inventory charges related to the discontinuation of our POLARx cryoablation system." (Executive VP & CFO Monson)
  • "First quarter adjusted operating margin was 28.0%." (Executive VP & CFO Monson)
  • "Free cash flow for the first quarter was $170 million" and "we now expect full year 2026 free cash flow to be approximately $4 billion." (Executive VP & CFO Monson)
  • "Our gross debt leverage ratio was 1.8x" as of March 31, 2026. (Executive VP & CFO Monson)

Q&A

  • Robert Marcus, JPMorgan Chase & Co.: what changed since the Q4 guide and why confidence in the long-range plan; Chairman, President & CEO Mahoney: "there's really 3 main contributors to the takedown of the guide... primarily EP, WATCHMAN and Urology," adding WATCHMAN softness first appeared "early to mid-February" and EP reflected "greater share erosion than we're particularly seeing."
  • Joanne Wuensch, Citigroup: quarterly phasing for EP/WATCHMAN/Urology; Chairman, President & CEO Mahoney: "We think second quarter is our toughest quarter of the year"; Executive VP & CFO Monson: "Global EP at 10% for the year implies mid- to high single-digit growth for the rest of the year" and WATCHMAN implies "low double-digit growth for the rest of the year" globally.
  • Larry Biegelsen, Wells Fargo: EP share pressure and whether U.S. EP stays flat; Chairman, President & CEO Mahoney: U.S. EP is guided to "mid-single-digit growth" for 2026, implying "a flat 2Q to 4Q," citing "increased competition" from "Medtronic," "J&J" and "Abbott."
  • Frederick Wise, Stifel: WATCHMAN post-CHAMPION reaction and how to address concerns; Chairman, President & CEO Mahoney: "We're seeing terrific concomitant demand" but "pressure" in stand-alone, and said actions include "commercial investments" and "marketing investments"; Senior VP & Global Chief Medical Officer Ken Stein: "trial kicked, all of its primary safety and efficacy endpoints" and "It just takes time for that to play through."
  • Joshua Jennings, TD Cowen: profit offsets given mix headwinds; Executive VP & CFO Monson: "we will see less mix benefit" and described "much more restrictive spend controls" and reviewing R&D to focus on "the most impactful programs."
  • Vijay Kumar, Evercore ISI: buyback rationale and funding while Penumbra pending; Executive VP & CFO Monson: "We intend to repurchase... $2 billion worth of shares" and "We'll fund that through cash on hand."
  • Matthew O'Brien, Piper Sandler: Penumbra transaction flexibility if cash component changes; Chairman, President & CEO Mahoney: "We're really excited about that team" and said the company is "hopeful and confident" on the shareholder vote.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts: slightly negative to skeptical, repeatedly pressing on the "takedown of the guide" and share/volume deceleration in "EP" and "WATCHMAN" (e.g., "what happened during first quarter that really prompted it?").
  • Management: slightly negative on near-term performance but confident on longer-term positioning, with Mahoney calling the revision "not my happiest moment" and "very disappointed," while also stating, "we remain convicted in the future of Boston Scientific." (Chairman, President & CEO Mahoney)
  • Versus the prior quarter, tone shifted from confidence in "10% plus" LRP sales to acknowledging LRP top-line pressure: "we're not going to make a comment on the LRP top line growth at this point." (Chairman, President & CEO Mahoney)

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Guidance changed from Q4’s "10% to 11%" full-year 2026 organic growth to Q1’s "6.5% to 8.0%," with management explicitly attributing the revision to "EP, WATCHMAN and Urology" and citing "unanticipated headwinds." (Chairman, President & CEO Mahoney)
  • EP messaging shifted from Q4’s expectation to "outpace" a ~15% EP market to Q1’s expectation that "we did lose a bit more share than we anticipated" and a revised view that "we now expect our global EP business to grow approximately 10% in 2026" with U.S. "mid-single-digit" growth. (Chairman, President & CEO Mahoney)
  • WATCHMAN commentary moved from Q4’s "outstanding 29% growth" to Q1’s "below our expectations" and a new full-year expectation: "we now expect global WATCHMAN growth to be mid-teens with low to mid-teens in the U.S." (Chairman, President & CEO Mahoney)

Risks and Concerns

  • "Unanticipated headwinds and changing business patterns" drove the guidance reset, centered on "EP, WATCHMAN and Urology." (Chairman, President & CEO Mahoney)
  • WATCHMAN risks cited included "hospital capacity, related procedure prioritization and evolving reimbursement dynamics." (Chairman, President & CEO Mahoney)
  • EP risks included "ongoing competitive dynamics" and expected "share erosion." (Chairman, President & CEO Mahoney)
  • Margin risks included "tariffs" and "inventory charges related to the discontinuation of our POLARx cryoablation system," with full-year gross margin expected "slightly below" 2025. (Executive VP & CFO Monson)

Final Takeaway

Management described Q1 as on-plan operationally (9.4% organic growth and $0.80 adjusted EPS) but reset 2026 expectations, guiding Q2 organic growth of 5% to 7% and full-year organic growth of 6.5% to 8% alongside $3.34 to $3.41 in adjusted EPS. Executives tied the revision primarily to WATCHMAN volume softness that emerged in mid-February, greater-than-expected EP share pressure amid intensifying competition, and slower Urology performance linked to stone management gaps and lingering sacral neuromodulation commercial disruption, while emphasizing actions spanning spend controls, targeted commercial and marketing investment, and continued commitment to the pipeline and planned Penumbra close in the second half of 2026.

data.title: Fundraising by direct lenders hits a three-year low in Q1
data.description:
Direct lending strategies saw their slowest fundraising activity in at least three years during the first quarter amid chaos in the private credit sector and Middle East war concerns, according to data from With Intelligence by S&P Global.

The lenders recorded $10.7B of final closes during the three months ended March 31, ~63% below the quarterly average of $28.7B seen between 2023 and 2025.

Comparatively, a total of $49.9B of private debt fundraising was tracked by With Intelligence in Q1.

Several funds delayed their first or final closes in recent weeks over market volatility resulting from the Iran conflict, the April 2 report noted, citing market participants.

Notable direct lending funds: (MSDL), (NCDL), (OBDC), (SAR)
